stem: Smoking, harmful alcohol use, physical inactivity and poor diet are most usefully grouped together as:
options: Wider determinants of health
Structural drivers of inequality
Environmental hazards
Health behaviours
rationale: These four are individual-level activities that influence health and are themselves shaped by social conditions, the standard meaning of health behaviours. Wider determinants are the broader social, economic and environmental conditions, not the behaviours. Structural drivers concern systemic processes that distribute advantage. Environmental hazards refer to physical exposures such as poor air quality, not behaviours.

stem: Smoking prevalence in the most deprived groups is consistently higher than in the least deprived. The clearest implication for inequalities is that smoking:
options: Is a major contributor to the gap in health outcomes between deprived and affluent groups
Has no role in health inequalities
Affects all social groups equally
Is the only behaviour that matters for inequalities
rationale: Because smoking is both harmful and more common in deprived groups, it is a substantial contributor to the difference in outcomes between deprived and affluent populations. It plainly has a role, so the first option is wrong. It does not affect all groups equally, which is the point. It is one important behaviour among several, not the only one.

stem: Why are health behaviours often described as 'socially patterned' rather than freely chosen?
options: Because they are determined entirely by genetics
Because everyone makes identical choices
Because their distribution follows social and economic circumstances, with damaging behaviours more common where conditions are harder
Because behaviours have no link to circumstances
rationale: Behaviours cluster along social lines because income, environment and opportunity shape what is feasible and likely, so they are patterned rather than freely chosen in a vacuum. They are not purely genetic. People do not make identical choices. The whole point is that behaviours are strongly linked to circumstances.

stem: The tendency for several harmful health behaviours to occur together in the same individuals is known as:
options: The prevention paradox
Risk clustering (multiple risk behaviours)
Regression to the mean
Confounding
rationale: When behaviours such as smoking, heavy drinking and inactivity co-occur in the same people, this is termed risk clustering or multiple risk behaviour. The prevention paradox concerns the distribution of benefit from preventive action. Regression to the mean is a statistical artefact of repeated measurement. Confounding is a distortion of an association by a common cause.

stem: Why does the clustering of risk behaviours matter particularly for health inequalities?
options: Because clusters are more common in disadvantaged groups and combine to multiply risk, widening between-group gaps
Clustering is spread evenly across all social groups
Because clustered behaviours cancel each other out
Because only one behaviour ever needs to be addressed
rationale: Risk behaviours cluster more in disadvantaged groups and their combined effect can exceed any single behaviour, so clustering widens the gap between groups. It is socially patterned, not evenly spread. The behaviours compound rather than cancel. Tackling only one leaves the others contributing to risk.

stem: A local authority public health team wants to reduce smoking-related inequalities. Consistent with the social patterning of behaviour, the most promising approach is to:
options: Rely solely on a mass-media campaign aimed equally at everyone
Assume the gap will close without specific action
Focus only on affluent areas where uptake of services is easiest
Combine population measures with targeted, well-resourced support in the most affected communities
rationale: Because smoking is patterned by circumstance, combining population-level measures with extra, well-resourced support where the burden is greatest matches effort to need. A uniform mass-media campaign tends to be taken up more by the advantaged and can widen the gap. Focusing on affluent areas neglects the groups with greatest need. Inaction will not close a patterned gap.

stem: Physical inactivity is shaped not only by individual motivation but also by the environment. Which factor is most likely to influence activity levels at population scale?
options: The colour of local signage
Genetic predisposition only
A person's daily intentions alone
The availability of safe, accessible places to walk, cycle and exercise
rationale: Environmental opportunities such as safe walking and cycling routes strongly shape activity across a population, linking the behaviour to environmental conditions. Individual intentions alone do not explain population patterns. Genetics does not account for socially patterned activity levels. Trivial features such as signage colour are not meaningful determinants.

stem: Why is framing health behaviours purely as 'lifestyle choices' criticised in public health?
options: Because behaviours are identical across all groups
Because behaviours have no effect on health
Because lifestyles are entirely fixed at birth
Because the word 'choice' understates how strongly behaviours are constrained by income, environment and opportunity
rationale: Calling behaviours 'choices' implies free individual selection and obscures the heavy influence of circumstance, which is why the framing is questioned. Behaviours do affect health. They are not fixed at birth. They differ systematically across groups, part of the very criticism.

stem: Harmful alcohol use shows a complex social gradient: the most deprived groups often suffer the greatest alcohol-related harm even where reported consumption is not the highest. This 'alcohol harm paradox' suggests that:
options: Deprivation protects against alcohol harm
Consumption and harm are unrelated
Factors such as co-occurring disadvantage, other behaviours and access to care modify how consumption translates into harm
Alcohol harm depends only on the amount consumed
rationale: The alcohol harm paradox indicates that the same drinking translates into greater harm in deprived groups because of compounding factors such as other risk behaviours, poorer baseline health and access to care. Harm is not a simple function of amount alone. Deprivation does not protect against harm. Consumption and harm are related, but not in a simple one-to-one way.

stem: A behaviour-change intervention has equal efficacy per participant in trials but widens inequalities when rolled out. The most likely reason is that:
options: The intervention does not work at all
Deprived groups always benefit more from interventions
Efficacy and uptake are unrelated
Uptake and adherence are higher among more advantaged groups, so realised benefit is unequal
rationale: Even an intervention with equal per-person efficacy can widen gaps if advantaged groups take it up and stick with it more, so benefit lands unequally. The premise of equal efficacy means it does work. Deprived groups do not automatically benefit more. Realised benefit depends on uptake as well as efficacy.

stem: Why is acting on a single behaviour (for example smoking) unlikely on its own to close health inequalities?
options: Behaviours are unrelated to one another
Single behaviours cannot be changed
Risk behaviours cluster and share upstream drivers, so addressing one leaves the others and their causes in place
Behaviour has no bearing on inequalities
rationale: Because behaviours co-occur and arise from shared upstream conditions, tackling one leaves the clustered others and their common causes operating, limiting impact on the gap. Behaviours are related through shared drivers. They can be changed. Behaviour does bear on inequalities, which is why a single-target approach falls short.

stem: Diet quality tends to be poorer in more deprived areas. Which explanation is most consistent with the determinants view of behaviour?
options: Diet quality is identical across all areas
Individuals in deprived areas simply prefer unhealthy food
Diet is determined solely by genetics
Cost, local food availability and time pressures constrain dietary choices alongside individual preference
rationale: The determinants view holds that diet is constrained by affordability, the local food environment and competing pressures, not by preference alone. Attributing it solely to preference ignores those constraints. Genetics does not explain area-level dietary patterns. Diet quality is not identical across areas, which is the observation being explained.

stem: Substance misuse is best understood within the determinants framework as a behaviour that:
options: Occurs randomly and is unrelated to social conditions
Is strongly associated with disadvantage, trauma and limited opportunity, while also affecting those conditions in turn
Has no health consequences
Affects only the most affluent groups
rationale: Substance misuse is patterned by disadvantage, adverse experiences and constrained opportunity, and can also feed back into those conditions, fitting the determinants view of behaviour. It is not random or unrelated to conditions. It does not affect only the affluent. It has clear health consequences.

stem: Why might a 'brief intervention' delivered opportunistically in services reach disadvantaged groups less effectively than intended?
options: Because brief interventions are always ineffective
Because brief interventions are only for affluent groups
Because those facing greater access barriers attend services less often, so opportunistic delivery can miss them
Because disadvantaged groups attend services more than anyone else
rationale: Opportunistic delivery depends on attendance, and groups facing greater access barriers attend less, so they can be under-reached, a manifestation of the inverse care pattern. Brief interventions are not inherently ineffective. Disadvantaged groups facing barriers attend less, not more. The interventions are not restricted to affluent groups.

stem: A public health team reframes a smoking strategy to address the conditions that make quitting harder in deprived areas, not just individual willpower. This shift reflects recognition that:
options: Behaviour is shaped by circumstances, so structural and environmental support improves the chances of change
Quitting is impossible in deprived areas
Willpower is the only determinant of quitting
Smoking is unrelated to deprivation
rationale: The reframing acknowledges that circumstances shape behaviour, so changing the surrounding conditions supports change rather than relying on willpower alone. Willpower is not the sole determinant. Smoking is clearly related to deprivation. Quitting is not impossible in deprived areas; it is made harder by conditions, which is what the strategy addresses.

stem: Income, employment and education are usually classified as:
options: Health behaviours
Clinical outcomes
Social and economic determinants of health
Biological risk factors
rationale: These are core social and economic determinants: structural conditions shaping opportunities and resources for health. They are not individual behaviours. They are not biological risk factors such as blood pressure. They are upstream conditions rather than clinical outcomes.

stem: Education influences health through several pathways. Which best captures why it is treated as a determinant?
options: It shapes later income, employment, health literacy and command over resources
It is the single determinant that overrides all others
It directly alters a person's genome
It affects health only during the school years
rationale: Education affects health via earning potential, type of work, health literacy and resources, which is why it counts as a determinant. It does not alter the genome. Its effects extend across the life course, not only school years. It is one important determinant among several, not an overriding one.

stem: Why is the quality and security of employment considered important for health, beyond income alone?
options: Because job security, control and working conditions independently affect health, so poor-quality work can damage health even when paid
Because income is unrelated to health
Because employment is a purely clinical variable
Because only unemployment ever affects health
rationale: Work affects health through security, control and conditions as well as pay, so poor-quality work can harm health despite providing income. Income is strongly related to health. Both poor work and unemployment matter. Employment is a social and economic determinant, not a clinical variable.

stem: Relative poverty, as opposed to absolute poverty, is particularly relevant to the social gradient because it:
options: Has no relationship with health
Reflects standing relative to wider society, aligning with the graded relationship between social position and health
Concerns only the inability to afford food and shelter
Is identical to absolute poverty
rationale: Relative poverty captures position relative to prevailing norms, matching how health is graded across the whole social distribution. Absolute poverty concerns basic needs and is a distinct concept. The two are not identical. Relative position is clearly linked to health through the gradient.

stem: Unemployment is associated with worse health. Which pathway is most consistent with the determinants literature?
options: Unemployment affects health only through reduced income
Unemployment has no measurable health effect
Unemployment improves health by reducing work stress
Loss of income, status, routine and social contact, together with increased stress, can all affect health
rationale: Unemployment acts through multiple pathways including lost income, status, routine, social contact and heightened stress, not income alone. It is not income-only. On balance it harms rather than improves health. It has clear measurable effects.

stem: A policy raising the minimum wage to improve living standards is, in the determinants framework, best characterised as:
options: An environmental intervention on air quality
An upstream, structural intervention acting on a social and economic determinant
A purely behavioural intervention
A downstream clinical intervention
rationale: Raising the minimum wage acts on income, a social and economic determinant, at policy level, making it upstream and structural. It is not clinical treatment, not behaviour change, and not an air-quality measure.

stem: Why is childhood poverty given particular weight in determinants analysis?
options: Because poverty in childhood has no lasting effect
Because childhood circumstances are easily reversed and so do not matter
Because early disadvantage can affect development, education and later opportunity, with effects across the life course
Because only adult poverty affects health
rationale: Childhood poverty can shape development, schooling and later opportunity, with consequences that persist across life, so it carries particular weight. It does have lasting effects. Adult poverty is not the only relevant period. Early circumstances are not trivially reversed, which is precisely why they matter.

stem: The observation that each step up the social ladder is associated with better health is best described as:
options: A social gradient driven substantially by social and economic position
A purely random pattern
An effect confined to the poorest group
A threshold effect
rationale: Stepwise improvement in health with rising social position is the social gradient, substantially driven by socioeconomic circumstances. It is not a single threshold. It is patterned, not random. It runs across all groups, not only the poorest.

stem: Why can debt and financial insecurity be considered determinants of health in their own right?
options: Because they have no psychological or material consequences
Because they affect only those on the highest incomes
Because they generate chronic stress and constrain access to the resources needed for health
Because money is irrelevant to health
rationale: Debt and insecurity produce sustained stress and limit the resources people can put towards health, acting as determinants. They have clear psychological and material consequences. They affect those on lower and middle incomes most, not only the highest. Money and material resources are clearly relevant to health.

stem: A determinant that raises the risk of many different conditions at once, such as low income, is described as having:
options: A generalised, non-specific effect across multiple outcomes
No relationship with health
A protective effect only
A highly specific effect on one disease
rationale: Major upstream determinants such as low income raise risk across many conditions rather than acting on one disease, a non-specific effect. This is the opposite of specificity. The effect is generally harmful at the disadvantaged end, not protective. It is clearly related to health.

stem: Which is the clearest example of acting on a social and economic determinant rather than a downstream factor?
options: Offering a brief leaflet on healthy eating
Expanding access to good-quality education and stable employment
Running a one-off exercise class
Prescribing medication to lower blood pressure
rationale: Expanding education and stable employment acts on upstream social and economic conditions, a determinant-level action. Prescribing, a leaflet and a one-off class all operate downstream at the individual level.

stem: Why might area-level income data understate the health effects of low income for some individuals?
options: Because area data are always fabricated
Because individuals within an area vary, so an area average can mask low-income people living in mixed or affluent areas
Because income never affects health
Because everyone in an area has the same income
rationale: Area averages summarise a geography, but individuals vary, so low-income people in mixed or affluent areas can be hidden by the average. Residents do not all share the same income. Income does affect health. Area data are not assumed fabricated; the issue is aggregation.

stem: The 'social determinants of health' are most accurately defined as:
options: The treatments available within the NHS
The genetic factors that determine disease
The conditions in which people are born, grow, live, work and age, and the forces shaping those conditions
The daily choices individuals make
rationale: Social determinants are the conditions of daily life together with the structural forces, such as the distribution of money, power and resources, that shape them. They are not genetic factors, a catalogue of treatments, or merely daily choices, though choices are influenced by them.

stem: Why is education sometimes described as having 'intergenerational' effects on health?
options: Because education has no link to health
Because a parent's education can influence the circumstances and opportunities of their children, affecting the children's later health
Because education affects only the individual educated
Because health is fixed at birth regardless of education
rationale: Parental education can shape household circumstances and children's opportunities, so its effects can carry across generations to influence the children's health. It is not confined to the individual educated. Education is clearly linked to health. Health is not fixed at birth irrespective of such conditions.

stem: A health and wellbeing board wants to act on the social and economic determinants of health. The most consistent action is to:
options: Restrict activity to clinical treatment pathways
Work with partners on income, employment, skills and education alongside health services
Distribute leaflets about healthy living
Focus only on hospital waiting times
rationale: Acting on social and economic determinants means cross-sector work on income, employment, skills and education through the partnership. Hospital waiting times, clinical pathways and leaflets all sit downstream of those conditions.

stem: Air quality, housing conditions, transport and access to green space are examples of:
options: Environmental determinants of health
Structural drivers of discrimination
Health behaviours
Population vulnerability factors
rationale: These are environmental determinants: features of the physical surroundings that influence health. They are not behaviours. Structural drivers refer to systemic processes that distribute advantage, such as discrimination. Vulnerability factors describe susceptibility characteristics such as isolation, not the environment itself.

stem: Poor housing affects health through multiple pathways at once. Which set best illustrates this?
options: Only the cost of rent
Solely the distance to the nearest hospital
Only the architectural style of the building
Cold and damp affecting respiratory health, overcrowding affecting infection and mental health, and insecurity affecting stress
rationale: Housing influences health along several pathways, including thermal and damp conditions, overcrowding and the psychosocial effects of insecurity, making it a powerful environmental determinant. Rent cost alone, distance to hospital alone, or superficial features do not capture the breadth of mechanisms.

stem: Why is exposure to outdoor air pollution considered an environmental determinant with implications for inequalities?
options: Because air pollution is distributed evenly across all areas
Because air quality has no health effects
Because only rural areas are affected
Because deprived communities are often more exposed to poor air quality, compounding other disadvantages
rationale: Air pollution affects respiratory and cardiovascular health and tends to be worse in deprived areas, so it compounds existing disadvantage and contributes to inequalities. It is not evenly distributed. It has clear health effects. It affects urban and roadside areas notably, not only rural ones.

stem: Access to green space is linked to better physical and mental health. In the determinants framework this is because green space:
options: Affects only the wealthiest residents
Has no measurable effect on health
Provides opportunities for activity, social contact and stress reduction within the living environment
Directly cures disease
rationale: Green space supports activity, social contact and stress recovery as a feature of the living environment, linking it to health. It does not directly cure disease. Its benefits are not confined to the wealthy; unequal access is itself an inequalities concern. It has measurable effects.

stem: The 'built environment' affects health partly by shaping behaviour. Which is the clearest example?
options: The genetic makeup of residents
Street layout and safe cycling infrastructure influencing how much people walk and cycle
The colour scheme of public buildings
The clinical coding system used by the local hospital
rationale: Street layout and safe cycling infrastructure shape active travel, a clear link between the built environment and behaviour. Genetics is not part of the built environment. Clinical coding and decorative colour schemes are not meaningful environmental determinants of behaviour.

stem: A local authority improving housing quality and expanding green space in deprived neighbourhoods is acting primarily on which class of determinant?
options: Genetic determinants
Health behaviours
Clinical determinants
Environmental determinants
rationale: Housing quality and green space are physical features of the living environment, so action on them targets environmental determinants. It is not direct behaviour change, genetic action, or clinical treatment.

stem: 'Deprivation amplification' describes how:
options: Poorer environments and fewer local resources can compound individual disadvantage, amplifying its health effects
Deprivation has no environmental component
Deprived areas always have better local resources
Affluent areas have worse facilities than deprived ones
rationale: Deprivation amplification is the idea that disadvantaged individuals often also live in resource-poor environments, so area and individual disadvantage compound one another. Deprived areas do not generally have better resources. Deprivation has clear environmental components. Affluent areas typically have better facilities, not worse.

stem: Why might improving the neighbourhood environment be expected to influence health behaviours?
options: Because environmental change affects only air pollution
Because behaviours are unaffected by surroundings
Because the environment shapes the opportunities and constraints within which behaviours occur
Because only clinical advice can change behaviour
rationale: Environmental features set the opportunities and constraints for behaviour, so safer, greener neighbourhoods make active travel and activity more feasible. Behaviours are affected by surroundings. Clinical advice is not the only lever. Environmental change influences more than air pollution alone.

stem: Fuel poverty, the inability to keep a home adequately warm at reasonable cost, is an environmental determinant relevant to inequalities because it:
options: Has no relationship to health
Affects only those on the highest incomes
Is purely a financial issue with no health dimension
Contributes to cold-related respiratory and cardiovascular harm, concentrated in low-income households
rationale: Fuel poverty links housing and income to cold-related respiratory and cardiovascular harm, falling most on low-income households, so it bridges environmental and economic determinants. It is clearly related to health. It affects low-income households most, not the highest earners. It has a definite health dimension.

stem: Transport is considered a determinant of health partly because it:
options: Is relevant only to clinical staff
Has no effect on health or access
Shapes access to work, services and social contact, and influences activity, road danger and air quality
Affects only journey times
rationale: Transport shapes access to opportunities and services and affects activity, road safety and air quality, making it a multifaceted determinant. It is more than journey times. It clearly affects health and access. It is relevant to the whole population, not only clinical staff.

stem: Overcrowded housing is associated with worse health outcomes mainly because it:
options: Has no plausible health pathway
Improves social support and so protects health
Affects only physical, never mental, health
Facilitates transmission of infection and contributes to stress and poorer mental health
rationale: Overcrowding raises infection transmission and contributes to stress and poorer mental health, explaining its association with worse outcomes. It does not protect health. It has clear health pathways. It affects both physical and mental health.

stem: Why are environmental determinants often described as offering 'population-level' levers for health?
options: Because they have no reach beyond single buildings
Because changes to shared environments such as air quality, housing standards and green space affect many people at once
Because they affect only individuals who opt in
Because they can only be changed one household at a time
rationale: Shared environmental features affect everyone exposed to them, so improving them reaches many people simultaneously, a population-level lever. They are not limited to one household at a time, do not require individual opt-in, and reach beyond single buildings.

stem: An analyst notes that two neighbourhoods with similar incomes differ markedly in health, and links this to differences in housing quality and green space. This illustrates that:
options: Environment plays no part once income is known
Environmental conditions can shape health independently of income
Income and environment are always identical
Health differences are purely random
rationale: Health differing between similarly-incomed areas with different environments shows the environment exerting an effect of its own. Environment does play a part beyond income. Income and environment are related but not identical. The patterned difference is not random.

stem: Which of the following best illustrates an environmental determinant acting as a structural inequality?
options: A clinician's choice of treatment
Major roads and polluting industry being sited disproportionately near deprived communities
A person choosing not to exercise
An individual's preference for indoor hobbies
rationale: The unequal siting of pollution sources near deprived communities is an environmental exposure distributed by structural processes, linking environment to inequality. Individual exercise choices and hobby preferences are behaviours, not structural environmental exposures. A clinician's treatment choice is a clinical decision.

stem: Why is access to affordable, healthy food sometimes treated as part of the environmental determinants of health?
options: Because diet is purely genetic
Because the local food environment, what is available, affordable and nearby, shapes diet alongside individual choice
Because food has no link to health
Because food availability is identical everywhere
rationale: The local food environment, including availability, affordability and proximity of healthy options, shapes diet as an environmental influence alongside personal choice. Food is clearly linked to health. Diet is not purely genetic. Food availability varies across areas, sometimes described in terms of food deserts.

stem: Deprivation, unequal access to resources, discrimination and systemic bias are best grouped as:
options: Clinical outcomes
Health behaviours
Environmental determinants
Structural drivers of inequality
rationale: These describe systemic processes that distribute advantage and disadvantage unequally across groups, the meaning of structural drivers of inequality. They are not behaviours, physical environmental features, or clinical outcomes.

stem: What most clearly distinguishes a 'structural' driver of inequality from an individual risk factor?
options: There is no meaningful distinction between them
Structural drivers are always biological
Structural drivers operate through systems, policies and social organisation rather than individual choice
Structural drivers affect only one person at a time
rationale: Structural drivers act through how society, its institutions and policies are organised, shaping conditions for whole groups, rather than through individual choices. They affect populations, not single individuals in isolation. They are social and systemic, not biological. The distinction is meaningful because it points to different levers for action.

stem: Why can discrimination be regarded as a determinant of health, not only a social injustice?
options: Because it affects only income
Because it is relevant only within clinical settings
Because it can shape access to resources, chronic stress exposure and the quality of care received, all affecting health
Because it has no measurable health consequences
rationale: Discrimination influences material resources, sustained stress and differential treatment in services, each with documented health effects, so it operates as a determinant. It has measurable consequences, extends well beyond income, and operates across many settings, not only clinical ones.

stem: Ethnic inequalities in health are best understood as arising substantially through:
options: Biology alone, fixed by ethnic group
Identical circumstances across all ethnic groups
Random variation with no pattern
Social and structural processes such as discrimination, deprivation and differential access
rationale: Ethnic inequalities operate largely through social and structural pathways including discrimination, deprivation and unequal access, rather than biology alone. They are not random. Ethnic groups differ in circumstances, which is why these pathways matter.

stem: Why is 'intersectionality' useful when analysing structural drivers of inequality?
options: Because social factors never interact
Because overlapping characteristics such as deprivation, ethnicity, gender and disability can combine to produce compounded disadvantage
Because it applies only to clinical coding
Because only one social factor ever affects health at a time
rationale: Intersectionality recognises that several characteristics can overlap and interact to create compounded disadvantage that single-axis analysis would miss. Health is shaped by multiple interacting factors. It is a social-analysis concept, not a coding convention.

stem: The 'inverse care law' states that:
options: Care should always be rationed by ability to pay
Good medical care tends to vary inversely with the need of the population served
More deprived areas always receive more health resource
Health need is inversely related to age
rationale: Tudor Hart's inverse care law observes that good care tends to be least available where need is greatest, often where market forces dominate. It is descriptive, not a prescription to ration by ability to pay. It does not claim deprived areas always get more resource, and it is not about age.

stem: A new beneficial service is taken up most in the least deprived areas and least in the most deprived. If unaddressed this pattern is most likely to:
options: Narrow existing health inequalities
Eliminate the social gradient
Widen existing health inequalities
Have no effect on inequalities
rationale: When a beneficial service reaches those with least need most and those with greatest need least, the gap tends to widen, an instance of the inverse care law. It will not narrow inequalities, is unlikely to leave them unchanged, and does not eliminate the gradient.

stem: 'Systemic bias' in a health or care system refers to:
options: Patterns built into processes, criteria or norms that produce unequal outcomes even without individual intent
Occasional mistakes by individual staff only
A statistical term for sampling error
Bias that affects only data storage
rationale: Systemic bias is embedded in processes, criteria and norms and can produce unequal outcomes without any individual intending harm. It is more than occasional individual error. It is not confined to data storage. It is not a sampling-error statistic.

stem: Why is unequal access to resources treated as a structural driver rather than an individual problem?
options: Because it has no health consequences
Because it affects only individuals who ask for help
Because access is identical for everyone
Because it is shaped by how resources, services and opportunities are distributed across society
rationale: Unequal access reflects the societal distribution of resources and opportunities, a structural matter rather than an individual failing. Access is not identical for everyone. It affects whole groups, not only those who ask. It has clear health consequences.

stem: The idea that socioeconomic advantage persists as a cause of inequalities because it provides flexible resources usable against whatever risks are current is captured by:
options: Fundamental cause theory
Lead-time bias
Regression to the mean
The ecological fallacy
rationale: Fundamental cause theory holds that higher social position confers adaptable resources, money, knowledge, power and connections, that protect health regardless of the specific risks of the day, so inequalities persist. Regression to the mean, the ecological fallacy and lead-time bias are unrelated statistical or inferential concepts.

stem: Why might inequalities re-emerge even after a specific health problem is solved?
options: Because inequalities are fixed and unchangeable
Because all groups have identical resources
Because solving problems has no effect
Because the underlying resource and power advantages persist and can reassert themselves through other conditions
rationale: If structural advantages in resources and power remain, inequalities can reappear via other conditions even after one problem is addressed, consistent with fundamental cause theory. Solving problems does have effects. Inequalities are not fixed. Groups do not have identical resources, which is the source of the persistence.

stem: A 'structural' intervention to reduce alcohol-related harm inequalities is best illustrated by:
options: Minimum unit pricing applied across the population
A poster campaign about safe drinking
A self-help leaflet
Brief advice during a GP consultation
rationale: Minimum unit pricing acts on population-wide conditions of access and affordability, a structural, upstream intervention. A poster campaign, brief advice and a leaflet all operate at the individual, downstream level.

stem: Why are structural drivers of inequality often described as requiring action 'beyond the health sector'?
options: Because they cannot be influenced by any policy
Because health services are irrelevant to inequalities
Because only the NHS can address them
Because the drivers lie in housing, employment, justice, education and the distribution of resources, which sit outside health services
rationale: Structural drivers are rooted in sectors such as housing, employment, justice and education and in resource distribution, so addressing them requires action across government, not health services alone. Health services are not irrelevant, but they cannot do it alone, and policy can influence these drivers.

stem: Power and the distribution of resources are central to structural explanations of inequality because they:
options: Determine how opportunities, services and protections are allocated across groups
Affect only those in formal politics
Have no bearing on who experiences advantage or disadvantage
Are relevant only to economic, never health, outcomes
rationale: Structural accounts place power and resource distribution at the centre because they govern how opportunities, services and protections are shared across groups, shaping health. They clearly bear on advantage and disadvantage. Their influence extends well beyond formal politics and beyond purely economic outcomes to health.

stem: Why can routinely collected service data sometimes obscure structural inequalities in access?
options: Because service data record everyone's need perfectly
Because structural inequalities do not exist in real systems
Because data are always deliberately falsified
Because those facing structural barriers may never reach services, so their unmet need does not appear in the data
rationale: Service data only capture need that reaches services, so structural barriers keeping people away leave their unmet need invisible, masking inequality. Service data do not record everyone's need. Structural inequalities are real. Data are not assumed falsified; the issue is who is missing from them.

stem: Social isolation, disability and access barriers are best classified as:
options: Environmental determinants
Clinical terminologies
Population vulnerability factors
Health behaviours
rationale: These are population vulnerability factors: characteristics or circumstances that increase a group's susceptibility to poor health or to the effects of other determinants. They are not behaviours, environmental features, or clinical coding systems.

stem: Why might two areas with similar deprivation scores still experience different health outcomes?
options: Differing concentrations of vulnerability factors such as isolation, disability or access barriers can modify the impact of deprivation
Outcomes are entirely random
Deprivation scores are always identical to outcomes
Vulnerability factors have no bearing on outcomes
rationale: Similarly deprived areas can diverge because vulnerability factors such as isolation, disability prevalence and access barriers shape how deprivation translates into outcomes. Deprivation and outcomes are related but not identical. Outcomes are patterned, not random. Vulnerability factors do bear on outcomes.

stem: 'Access barriers' as a vulnerability factor are best understood as:
options: A purely clinical measurement
The range of obstacles, physical, financial, informational and cultural, that impede a group's use of services or resources
Barriers affecting all groups equally
Only the physical distance to a hospital
rationale: Access barriers span distance, cost, information, language, culture and trust, any of which can prevent a group obtaining services or resources. They are not a clinical measurement. They fall unequally across groups, which is why they contribute to inequalities.

stem: Why is social isolation considered a vulnerability factor relevant to health?
options: Because it is purely a clinical diagnosis
Because it has no effect on health
Because it is associated with worse physical and mental health and can reduce access to support and help-seeking
Because it affects only the wealthiest groups
rationale: Isolation is linked to worse physical and mental health and can limit support and help-seeking, making it a vulnerability factor. It does have health effects. It is not confined to the wealthiest. It is a social circumstance rather than a clinical diagnosis, though it affects clinical outcomes.

stem: People with disabilities can experience compounded health disadvantage. Which explanation is most consistent with the determinants view?
options: Barriers in environments, services and employment can add to the direct effects of a condition, worsening outcomes
Outcomes for disabled people are identical to the general population
Disability has no relationship to wider determinants
Disability affects only the most affluent
rationale: Environmental, service and employment barriers can compound the direct effects of a condition, so disabled people face added disadvantage, a determinants-based explanation. Disability interacts strongly with wider determinants. It does not affect only the affluent. Outcomes are not identical to the general population, which is the inequality being explained.

stem: Older people living alone with limited mobility may form a particularly vulnerable group because:
options: They always have the best health outcomes
Combined factors such as isolation, reduced mobility and access barriers can amplify the effect of other determinants
Vulnerability factors cancel each other out
Age and isolation never interact
rationale: When isolation, reduced mobility and access barriers combine, they can amplify the impact of other determinants, marking out a vulnerable group. Age and isolation do interact. Such groups do not automatically have the best outcomes. Vulnerability factors compound rather than cancel.

stem: 'Inclusion health' groups, such as people experiencing homelessness or those with insecure immigration status, are characterised by:
options: Being identical to the general population in risk
Extreme exposure to multiple vulnerability factors and very poor health outcomes, often missed by routine data
Excellent access to services and very good health
Having no distinctive health needs
rationale: Inclusion health groups face overlapping vulnerabilities and markedly poor outcomes, and are frequently under-represented in routine data, complicating needs assessment. They typically have poor access and health, are not identical in risk to the general population, and have distinctive needs.

stem: Resilience, in the context of health inequalities, is best understood as:
options: A reason to stop addressing structural causes
The capacity of individuals or communities to maintain or recover health despite adversity, shaped by resources and conditions
The complete absence of adversity
A fixed personality trait that cannot change
rationale: Resilience is the ability to cope with and recover from adversity, shaped by available resources and conditions rather than a fixed trait. It is not unchangeable. Recognising it does not justify ignoring structural causes. It describes coping with adversity, not its absence.

stem: Why is it problematic to frame health inequalities purely as a matter of individual resilience?
options: Because it can shift responsibility onto individuals and away from the structural conditions producing the adversity
Because resilience does not exist
Because resilience applies only to clinicians
Because resilience is the only thing that matters
rationale: Over-emphasising individual resilience risks blaming people for circumstances created upstream, diverting attention from structural action. Resilience does exist but is not the sole factor. It is not a clinician-only concept. The concern is misattributed responsibility, not the existence of resilience.

stem: A community with strong social networks may show better-than-expected health for its deprivation level. This is most consistent with:
options: Social capital acting as a protective resource
The ecological fallacy
Confounding by indication
Lead-time bias
rationale: Social capital, the networks, trust and reciprocity within a community, can buffer some effects of deprivation, explaining better-than-expected outcomes. The ecological fallacy is an inferential error, lead-time bias a screening artefact and confounding by indication a treatment-study bias, none of which describes a protective community resource.

stem: Why are some vulnerability factors described as 'intersecting' with deprivation rather than acting alone?
options: Because they have no relationship to deprivation
Because they only ever affect affluent groups
Because factors such as disability, isolation and language barriers often co-occur with and compound deprivation
Because they always occur in isolation
rationale: Vulnerability factors frequently co-occur with deprivation and compound it, so they intersect rather than acting alone. They do not occur in isolation. They affect disadvantaged groups particularly. They are clearly related to deprivation.

stem: Why can relying on registered service users alone underestimate need among vulnerable groups?
options: Because registration data are always complete
Because vulnerable groups always register and attend reliably
Because those facing the greatest barriers are least likely to be registered or to attend, so their need is hidden
Because vulnerable groups have no health needs
rationale: The most vulnerable often face the greatest barriers to registering and attending, so their need is least visible in user-based data, leading to underestimation. They do not register and attend reliably. They do have health needs. Registration data are frequently incomplete for these groups.

stem: Carers (people providing unpaid care) are sometimes identified as a vulnerable group because:
options: The demands of caring can affect carers' physical and mental health, finances and access to support
Caring affects only the person receiving care
Caring has no effect on the carer's own health
Carers always have better health than non-carers
rationale: Unpaid caring can damage the carer's own physical and mental health, finances and access to support, marking carers as a vulnerable group. Caring does affect the carer. Carers do not reliably have better health. The effects extend to the carer, not only the cared-for person.

stem: Why is identifying vulnerability factors important when planning services to reduce inequalities?
options: Because vulnerability is irrelevant to service design
Because it removes the need for any data
Because it allows services to be designed to reach and support groups who would otherwise be missed
Because it guarantees identical outcomes for everyone
rationale: Recognising vulnerability factors lets planners design services that reach and support otherwise-missed groups, an equity-oriented step. Vulnerability is highly relevant to design. It does not guarantee identical outcomes. It complements rather than replaces data.

stem: Children in care and care leavers are often considered a vulnerable group because they:
options: Experience elevated rates of adverse health, educational and social outcomes linked to early disadvantage and disruption
Have identical outcomes to the general population
Have no distinctive needs
Are protected from all health risks
rationale: Children in care and care leavers show elevated adverse outcomes tied to early disadvantage and disruption, marking them as vulnerable. Their outcomes are not identical to the general population. They are not protected from all risk. They have distinctive needs that services must consider.

stem: Which determinant is the single largest preventable contributor to the gap in life expectancy between the most and least deprived groups in England?
options: Sunlight exposure
Tea consumption
Left-handedness
Smoking
rationale: Smoking is consistently identified as the largest single preventable contributor to the life-expectancy gap between deprived and affluent groups, because it is both harmful and far more common in deprived populations. Sunlight exposure, handedness and tea consumption are not major drivers of the gap.

stem: Long-term exposure to cold, damp housing is most strongly linked to which group of outcomes?
options: Better mental health
Reduced rates of all infections
Respiratory conditions and cardiovascular strain
Improved cardiovascular fitness
rationale: Cold and damp housing is associated with respiratory conditions, such as worsened asthma and chest infections, and with cardiovascular strain from cold exposure. It does not improve fitness, reduce infections, or benefit mental health; insecure or poor housing tends to worsen mental health.

stem: Physical inactivity and obesity are most directly associated with an increased risk of which condition?
options: Short-sightedness
Hay fever
Type 2 diabetes
Colour blindness
rationale: Physical inactivity and obesity are well-established risk factors for type 2 diabetes, alongside cardiovascular disease and some cancers. Short-sightedness, hay fever and colour blindness are not outcomes meaningfully driven by inactivity or obesity.

stem: Harmful and sustained alcohol consumption is most strongly linked to which outcome?
options: Better sleep quality
Liver disease
Improved liver function
Reduced cancer risk
rationale: Sustained harmful drinking is a leading cause of liver disease and also raises the risk of several cancers and cardiovascular harm. It does not improve liver function, reduce cancer risk, or improve sleep; alcohol tends to disrupt sleep.

stem: Outdoor air pollution (for example fine particulate matter) is most consistently associated with increased rates of:
options: Improved lung capacity
Respiratory and cardiovascular disease
Reduced asthma prevalence
Better birth outcomes
rationale: Fine particulate air pollution is consistently linked to respiratory and cardiovascular disease, and to adverse rather than improved birth outcomes. It does not improve lung capacity or reduce asthma; exposure tends to worsen these.

stem: The strong link between low income and worse health across many conditions is best interpreted as evidence of:
options: Income having no causal role
A purely genetic phenomenon
A coincidence requiring no explanation
A common upstream cause, supporting action on the structural determinant rather than each disease in isolation
rationale: When low income tracks worse outcomes across many distinct conditions, a shared upstream driver is the most parsimonious explanation, strengthening the case for structural action. It is not mere coincidence. It is consistent with income having a causal role. A purely genetic account cannot explain a socially patterned, cross-condition association.

stem: Early-life disadvantage (for example child poverty) is linked to poorer adult health partly through:
options: An effect confined entirely to infancy
No plausible mechanism at all
A guarantee of poor health regardless of later conditions
Effects on development, education and later opportunity that accumulate across the life course
rationale: Early disadvantage shapes development, schooling and later opportunity, with effects that accumulate over the life course to influence adult health. It does not guarantee poor health irrespective of later conditions, is not confined to infancy, and operates through well-described mechanisms.

stem: Maternal smoking during pregnancy is most directly associated with which outcome?
options: Low birth weight and related complications
Improved foetal lung development
Reduced risk of all childhood illness
Increased average birth weight
rationale: Smoking in pregnancy is strongly linked to low birth weight and associated complications. It does not raise birth weight, reduce childhood illness, or improve foetal lung development; it impairs them.

stem: Why is the relationship between determinants and outcomes often described as 'graded' rather than 'all-or-nothing'?
options: Because risk tends to rise incrementally across the whole spectrum of exposure or social position, not only at an extreme
Because outcomes are unrelated to determinants
Because only the most exposed group is ever affected
Because the relationship is purely random
rationale: Determinant-outcome relationships typically show incremental change across the whole spectrum, mirroring the social gradient, rather than a single cut-point. Outcomes are clearly related to determinants. More than the most-exposed group is affected. The relationship is patterned, not random.

stem: A determinant influencing an outcome through an intermediate factor, such as deprivation affecting respiratory disease partly via higher smoking rates, illustrates:
options: An absence of any relationship
A pathway in which an intermediate factor carries part of the effect from determinant to outcome
A direct effect with no intermediate steps
An outcome causing the determinant
rationale: Here deprivation acts partly through an intermediate factor (smoking) that carries some of the effect to the outcome, illustrating a determinant-to-outcome pathway. It is not a direct, step-free effect. The causal direction runs from determinant to outcome, not the reverse. There is a clear relationship.

stem: Mental ill-health shows a strong social gradient. Which determinant set is most consistently associated with higher rates of common mental disorders?
options: Regular physical activity
Strong social networks and stable housing
Financial insecurity, unemployment and social isolation
High income and secure employment
rationale: Financial insecurity, unemployment and isolation are consistently associated with higher rates of common mental disorders. High income, secure work, strong networks, stable housing and activity are generally protective, not risk-raising.

stem: Why does a determinant such as deprivation often appear to affect 'healthy life expectancy' more than overall life expectancy?
options: Because the two measures are identical
Because deprived groups tend to spend a greater proportion of a shorter life in poor health, widening the healthy-life-expectancy gap
Because deprived groups live longer in good health
Because deprivation has no effect on either measure
rationale: Deprivation tends to compress healthy years more than total years, so the gap in healthy life expectancy is typically wider than the gap in life expectancy. Deprivation affects both measures. Deprived groups do not live longer in good health. The two measures are distinct, which is why they diverge.

stem: The clustering of cardiovascular risk factors (smoking, poor diet, inactivity, high blood pressure) in deprived groups helps explain why:
options: Cardiovascular disease contributes substantially to the life-expectancy gap between deprived and affluent groups
Cardiovascular disease affects only affluent groups
Cardiovascular disease shows little social variation
Risk factors never combine
rationale: Because cardiovascular risk factors cluster in deprived groups, cardiovascular disease is a major contributor to the deprivation gap in life expectancy. It shows marked social variation, not little. It does not affect only affluent groups. Risk factors do combine, which is the point.

stem: When interpreting a determinant-outcome association from area-level data, a key caution is that:
options: Inferring individual risk directly from area-level associations risks the ecological fallacy
Area data cannot show any association
Individual and area data are interchangeable
Area associations always hold at the individual level
rationale: Concluding individual-level risk straight from an area-level association risks the ecological fallacy, since within-area variation can differ from the aggregate pattern. Area associations do not always hold for individuals. Individual and area data are not interchangeable. Area data can certainly show associations; the caution is about interpreting them.

stem: Why is it useful to prioritise a small set of high-burden, strongly-gradiented conditions when analysing determinant-outcome links for a population?
options: Because rare conditions are the only ones that matter
Because every possible link must be analysed at once to be valid
Because the choice of conditions makes no difference
Because focusing on conditions that are common, high-burden and socially patterned yields the most actionable insight for reducing inequalities
rationale: Concentrating on common, high-burden, strongly-gradiented conditions targets analysis where it can most inform action on inequalities. Analysing every possible link at once is neither feasible nor necessary. Rare conditions are not the priority for population inequalities. The choice of conditions clearly affects how actionable the analysis is.
